Call 2025 - Round 3
Mobility of young people (KA152-YOU)
Form ID: KA152-YOU-462DFCE7 Deadline (Brussels Time): 01 Oct 2025 12:00:00
Project Summary
Please provide short answers to the following questions to summarise the information you have provided in the rest of the
application form.
Please use full sentences and clear language. In case your project is accepted, the summary you provided will be made
public by the European Commission and the National Agencies.
What do you want to achieve by implementing the project? What are the objectives of your project? Please specify from the
perspective of youth work practice.
This project aims to help young people develop a deeper understanding and appreciation of cultural diversity while learning
how to overcome language barriers. By exploring myths and traditional stories from different cultures, participants will reflect
on the values, beliefs and perspectives that shape identities and influence the way people communicate. Storytelling will be
used as a tool to facilitate dialogue, allowing participants to express their own cultural backgrounds while discovering
connections with others. Through interactive discussions, collaborative activities and creative experimentation, they will
develop the ability to critically explore different forms of communication, both verbal and non-verbal. By working in an
international environment, they will learn to listen actively, interpret meaning beyond words and adapt their communication
styles to different cultural contexts. This experience will not only improve their intercultural awareness, but also strengthen
their empathy and ability to challenge stereotypes. Through storytelling activities, they will be empowered to find creative
and inclusive ways to share their perspectives while respecting those of others. Beyond the workshops, the experience will
provide valuable skills that can be applied in everyday, educational and professional settings. Participants will improve their
ability to work together, develop confidence in expressing themselves in multilingual and multicultural environments, and
gain insights into how communication can be a bridge rather than a barrier.
What activities do you plan to implement? What is the number and profile of the participants involved?
The project will involve 32 young participants, 4 team leaders, and 2 facilitators from 4 different countries: Hungary, Estonia,
Italy, and Denmark. In each team, we plan to include 2 participants primarily with disadvantaged economic backgrounds.
The participants will come from different cultural and linguistic backgrounds, creating an environment in which a wide range
of perspectives can be shared and explored. The young participants will be between 18 and 30 years old, while the team
leaders will be at least 18 years old and have previous experience in Erasmus+ or local youth work. Participants will be
selected based on their motivation for the topic, while respecting gender balance and giving priority to those with fewer
opportunities
The project will take participants from initial exploration to the final presentation of their work. Activities will begin with
interactive sessions designed to build trust and encourage collaboration, creating an environment where participants feel
comfortable sharing their perspectives. Then, through discussion and storytelling exercises, they will explore myths and
legends from different cultures, reflecting on their deeper meanings and the values they convey. These exchanges will
highlight both the uniqueness and the similarities of cultural narratives, creating a sense of connection and mutual
understanding. The role of non-verbal communication will be a key focus, with dedicated workshops exploring how music,
visual and body language can enrich narratives and transmit emotions beyond words. As participants become more familiar
with the theme, they will begin to design their own creative projects. Working in teams, they will choose a story to reimagine
using digital tools, exploring how elements such as structure and character development contribute to effective storytelling.
Once the stories take shape, participants will prepare for the final showcase, working on their presentation skills to ensure
they can effectively communicate their creative vision and engage their audience. The project will conclude with the
presentation of the completed digital stories, giving participants the opportunity to celebrate their achievements and reflect
on their learning journey. A structured discussion will follow, encouraging them to consider the wider impact of storytelling on
intercultural dialogue and media literacy. The final moments of the project will focus on reinforcing the sense of community
that has been built, leaving participants with a shared sense of achievement and inspiration for the future.
The most important workshops of the project are:
Myths and Storytelling in Different Cultures
Participants explore myths and legends through a “world café” discussion. Small groups rotate between theme tables,
sharing stories from their cultures and reflecting on their meaning and values. Table hosts summarise insights, leading to a
collective debate. The session support intercultural awareness, analytical thinking, and appreciation of storytelling traditions.
Telling a Story Without Words:
In small groups, participants create and perform short stories using only movement, facial expressions, gestures, or objects.
After each performance, the audience reflects on what they understood, highlighting the power of nonverbal storytelling as a
universal communication tool.
Creating a Mini Visual Story:
Teams design short digital stories on a chosen theme, collaborating on creative decisions and message clarity. The activity
develops digital skills, teamwork, and creativity while encouraging cross-cultural communication.

Introduction to Digital Storytelling Tools:
This workshop introduces participants to simple, accessible tools for digital storytelling, starting with a brief look at their role
in intercultural communication. Through guided demonstrations and small-group practice, participants will learn to use free
platforms such as Canva, Audacity, Pictory, and Storybird for creating visual, audio, and narrative content. The aim is to
build practical skills and confidence, encouraging experimentation and collaborative learning for later project phases.
Music as a Universal Communication Tool:
This workshop explores how music expresses emotions, tells stories, and connects people beyond language. After a short
introduction with cultural examples, participants experiment with simple instruments and digital tools to create sounds and
rhythms, playfully discovering elements they can later use in digital stories.
The Value of Storytelling in Intercultural Dialogue:
Through an “interactive gallery walk,” participants reflect on storytelling’s role in promoting dialogue and empathy.
Responding to guiding questions, they share insights on posters and later discuss common themes, strengthening
understanding of storytelling as a tool for intercultural connection.
What results and impact do you expect your project to have?
This project aims to have a lasting impact on participants by equipping them with the skills and awareness needed for
meaningful intercultural dialogue. Through storytelling, they will develop a deeper understanding of different cultural
narratives and learn how to use digital tools to communicate these stories effectively. While the digital stories produced will
be a tangible outcome, the real impact will be seen in the personal development of the participants and their ability to apply
what they have learned outside of the project. By exploring myths, legends and non-verbal communication, participants will
move beyond superficial representations of cultural diversity and become more aware of how identities are formed and learn
to appreciate both differences and similarities. The project will also strengthen participants' communication skills, both
verbal and non-verbal, while increasing their confidence in intercultural settings. The use of digital storytelling will develop
their media literacy and creative thinking, providing practical skills useful in education, youth work and the creative
industries. Working with peers from different backgrounds will enhance their teamwork and problem-solving skills, preparing
them for diverse social and professional environments, as, beyond the individual impact, some may use storytelling for
social inclusion, while others may integrate non-verbal communication techniques into educational or creative projects. In
the long term, we expect the project to have a strong impact on participants’ personal and professional growth, particularly
in the areas of intercultural communication and storytelling.
In the long term, the project also aims to encourage participants to become more active citizens, take the lead in initiatives
within their own communities, and be aware of the opportunities offered by the European Union for young people. We expect
a broader societal impact among participants communities, as we would like participants to be ambassadors of the project
and use the skills and knowledge they gained in the future.
An important, lasting impact of the project could also be the collaboration between the partner organizations, which could
lead to a larger-scale project building on the lessons learned from this project in the same field.

What are the activities and experience of the organisation in youth work? Please provide information on your organisation’s
/ group’s regular youth work activities.
Square of Youth Association (SOYA) is a youth-led organisation where all activities are designed, planned, and
implemented by young people. Our work focuses on youth entrepreneurship, inclusion, sustainability, and active
participation in international mobility programs, providing young people with opportunities to develop skills, broaden their
perspectives, and take part in meaningful learning experiences.
Regarding entrepreneurship, SOYA implemented the "Entrepreneur in a Week" Erasmus+ Youth Exchange in April 2025.
Participants gained essential skills and knowledge to develop entrepreneurial ideas, including business plan writing,
pitching, and financing. The project encouraged young people to explore their potential and take initiative in their personal
and professional growth.
We are also implemented a Visegrad V4 Gen Mini-Grant project: Policy-Making Hackathon for Young Entrepreneurs, which
took place in Budapest from March 5 to 9, 2025. This initiative brought together young people for a 5-day policy-making
hackathon, where they analyzed the barriers to entrepreneurship and propose solutions. The outcomes of this project
compiled into a policy document and presented to decision-makers and stakeholders, ensuring that young voices contribute
to shaping policies that support youth entrepreneurship.
SOYA is strongly committed to inclusion and intercultural exchange. Our ESC-30 Solidarity project, "Board Games for
Inclusion", focuses on bringing together Hungarian and international students in Budapest through board games. This
initiative creates an informal space where young people from different backgrounds can meet and play together. The project
started in September 2024 and became a regular local activity of SOYA in Budapest, helping foreign young people settle
into their new surroundings while providing local students with opportunities to engage in intercultural experiences.
We also play an active role in democratizing access to Erasmus+ opportunities. Many young people are unaware of the
different mobility programs available to them, so we organize Erasmus+ information sessions that introduce them to various
possibilities, including Erasmus for Young Entrepreneurs and the European Solidarity Corps (ESC). By providing clear and
accessible guidance, we aim to ensure that young people from all backgrounds can take advantage of these opportunities.
As part of our outreach efforts, SOYA exhibited at Sziget Festival 2024 and 2025, engaging directly with festival-goers to
promote Erasmus+ through interactive and engaging activities. Our presence at the festival allowed us to connect with a
broad international audience, answer questions about mobility programs, and encourage young people to explore new
opportunities. Moving forward, we aim to expand our presence at festivals and public events, ensuring that Erasmus+

Background and experience
Please briefly present the organisation / the group
CSV Brescia is the Volunteer Service Center for the province of Brescia, founded in 1997 from the merger of two local
entities: Unione Centro Servizi and Organizzazione Centro Servizi. CSV Brescia operates with the mission of promoting and
supporting volunteerism and civic engagement throughout Brescia and its province. The organization provides support to
associations and citizens through a wide range of services and initiatives.
Since 2020, CSV Brescia has been registered in the Lombardy section of the Universal Civil Service Register.
In 2024, CSV Brescia obtained the Quality Label as a Lead, Supporting, and Hosting Organization within the European
Solidarity Corps (ESC) program.
CSV Brescia’s Key Activities:
• Volunteer Promotion: Through awareness campaigns and guidance services aimed at citizens, especially young people.
The volunteering and Universal & European Civil Service (ESC) orientation desk serves as a key reference point for those
interested in personal growth and active citizenship.
• Training and Professional Development: Offering courses and workshops for volunteers, leaders, and staff of Third Sector
organizations, covering topics such as legal and administrative matters, as well as social project development.
• Consulting and Technical-Logistical Support: Assisting organizations with establishment, management, fundraising,
initiative planning, and access to technical and logistical resources for events and activities.
• Project Development and Innovation: Leading innovative projects that engage local communities, schools, and other
regional entities. CSV Brescia also supports organizations in participating in funding programs such as Erasmus+ and the
European Solidarity Corps (ESC).
• Community Engagement and Events: Organizing meetings, initiatives, and events to foster a culture of volunteerism,
solidarity, and community spirit.
Through these activities, CSV Brescia serves as a key reference point for associations, volunteers, and citizens committed
to improving society and strengthening local communities. CSV Brescia's actions are guided by the Charter of Volunteering
Values and the Charter of Representation.
As of December 31, 2022, CSV Brescia's membership consisted of:
• 120 Volunteer Organizations
• 43 Other Third Sector Entities (including 6 Non-Profit Organizations)

What are the activities and experience of the partner organisation in youth work? Please provide information on your

CSV Brescia has extensive experience in promoting volunteering, active citizenship, and youth worker training, with a
particular focus on the Third Sector and the empowerment of local communities. The organization supports volunteers and
associations through training, consulting, community engagement, and guidance services, fostering the development of
transferable skills and innovative methodologies in youth work.
In particular, CSV Brescia is actively involved in designing and implementing educational initiatives based on non-formal
education, youth participation, and the development of inclusive and sustainable practices. The organization has gained
specialized expertise in managing learning pathways for youth
workers, promoting the enhancement of their methodological skills and the creation of collaborative networks between local
and international organizations.
Since 2018, CSV Brescia has been working alongside Atelier Europeo to promote the European Solidarity Corps (ESC)
program and volunteer projects, supporting the dissemination of over 70 outgoing volunteering projects and the
management of 3 hosting projects (Gori and Bergamo).
Since 2020, CSV Brescia has been registered in the Lombardy section of the Universal Civil Service Register. Through this
experience, the organization has developed strong expertise in managing learning experiences for young volunteers,
providing specific training, organizational support, and tools
for their personal and professional growth.
In 2024, CSV Brescia obtained the Quality Label as a Lead, Supporting, and Hosting Organization within the European
Solidarity Corps (ESC) program. The association has already facilitated the departure of ESC volunteers abroad and is
currently planning the hosting of European volunteers at its facilities starting in May 2025. This process involves a structured
management of the entire project cycle, from initial orientation and pre-departure training to ongoing support throughout the
volunteering experience and final impact evaluation.
Through these experiences, CSV Brescia has strengthened its administrative and organizational capacity in managing
international projects, ensuring adequate support for participants and a positive impact on the communities involved.
Thanks to its network of local and international partners, CSV Brescia offers a dynamic and stimulating learning
environment, fostering the exchange of best practices and the dissemination of innovative methodologies in youth work.
Please give information on the key staff/persons involved in this application and on the competences and previous
experience that they will bring to the project.
The staff members of CSV Brescia are experts in administrative procedures, effective partner communication, and
participant support. They are highly skilled in managing requests from volunteers, trainers, and facilitators, ensuring a
smooth and professional project implementation process. CSV Brescia stands out for its ability to oversee, monitor, and
evaluate all project phases, creating an environment that fosters learning, intercultural cooperation, and inclusivity.

Project Rationale
In this section, you are asked to explain the aims of your project, what you want to achieve and what are its expected
benefits on participants and participating organisations. Make sure to read the questions carefully and address all sub-
questions.
Needs and Objectives
Why do you want to carry out this project? Please describe the issues and needs you want to address and your project’s
objectives.
In our increasingly globalised world, cultural diversity is more visible than ever, yet true intercultural dialogue remains a
challenge. Stereotypes, misinformation and superficial representations often shape young people's perceptions of different
cultures, leading to misunderstandings and a lack of real connection. At the same time, digital media play an important role
in shaping narratives, but young people are rarely equipped with the tools to use them responsibly. This project seeks to
address these issues by combining traditional storytelling and digital creativity to promote deeper intercultural
understanding. Myths and legends are not just stories: they reflect the beliefs, values and histories of different communities.
By exploring and adapting these stories, participants will reflect on their own cultural backgrounds while discovering
common themes that connect them to others. They will also explore the power of non-verbal communication, such as music,
visuals and body language as universal means of expression that transcend language barriers. Through practical
workshops, discussion and collaboration, they will develop skills in critical thinking, media literacy and digital storytelling,
while learning how to identify and challenge stereotypes, communicate across cultural boundaries and create narratives that
promote inclusivity and mutual respect.
This project is significant for our organisation because it is in line with our mission to empower young people to become
active citizens and contribute to their community. This project addresses this through creativity, dialogue and inclusion. It
provides an opportunity to strengthen our role as a space for intercultural exchange, and to develop innovative educational
practices using digital tools.
How does your project link to the objectives of the Erasmus programme and those of Youth Exchanges?
This project embodies the core principles of Erasmus+ and Youth Exchanges, in particular by promoting intercultural
learning, active participation and digital creativity. Through storytelling, participants will reflect on cultural diversity and share
perspectives, leading to a deeper understanding of their own identity and the experiences of others, reinforcing the
Erasmus+ objective of strengthening social cohesion and mutual respect. By working in teams and participating in practical
activities, participants will develop key competences as the project promotes personal development and social inclusion by
providing young people with critical thinking, problem solving and communication skills. It also promotes active citizenship
by exploring how narratives influence perceptions, and how storytelling can become a tool for challenging stereotypes. In
addition, the project supports the development of digital skills: nowadays, media literacy and digital storytelling are essential
for self-expression and participation in society. Furthermore, the exchange will bring together young people from different
backgrounds, creating an environment where they can collaborate and find new ways to engage with cultural narratives.
The emphasis on storytelling as a universal form of communication ensures that all participants, regardless of their language
skills, can actively contribute, making the experience truly inclusive.
The project also promotes intercultural communication and the encounter and understanding of different cultures, for
example through intercultural evenings, numerous tasks based on international teamwork, and the meeting of young people
from four different countries with diverse backgrounds. Additionally, it fosters a sense of European spirit and belonging to
the European Union by bringing together different European cultures and through activities such as the Erasmus+ info
session, also integrating YouthPass into the youth exchange.
Impact

This project will help participants to become more aware of different cultures and to develop a deeper understanding of
intercultural communication. By exploring myths and stories from different traditions, they will reflect on the values and
beliefs that shape people's identities as they will share their own cultural backgrounds and discover similarities and
differences with others in a respectful way. During the project they will work in international teams, take part in storytelling
activities and learn how to communicate not only through words but also through music, images and body language. This
will help them build empathy, improve their communication skills and feel more confident in a multicultural environment.
After the project, participants will be able to use the skills and knowledge they have gained, such as teamwork, creativity
and digital tools, in their studies, personal lives and future careers. The experience will inspire them to see cultural diversity
as a strength and to contribute to a more inclusive society. Moreover, taking part in an Erasmus+ youth exchange is a
unique opportunity for young people to grow on both personal and social level. It helps them to develop key soft skills such

What are the basic elements of the activity? Please describe at the very least the venue(s), non formal learning methods
used, aims of the session etc.
The activity will take place at Legányi Apartmanház in Eger. We chose Eger as the location also because the city is symbolic
in the sense that cultures and religions meet here. In addition, it is an excellent venue for the youth exchange; we have
already organized a youth exchange here before, so it is a tested and proven location. The entire venue would be exclusively
ours, so our project would not disturb others. The apartment house provides full catering, taking into account the
participants’ needs regarding different diets and food allergies. It also has a large hall where we can hold the sessions. The
apartment house is easily accessible by public transport, just a 15-minute walk from the train station.
The entire timetable has been structured in a project-based way, where participants can create their own digital story
through the power of non-formal education, while also getting to know the stories and cultures of others. Here are the most
important sessions:
Myths and Storytelling in Different Cultures
Participants explore myths and legends through a “world café” discussion. Small groups rotate between theme tables,
sharing stories from their cultures and reflecting on their meaning and values. Table hosts summarise insights, leading to a
collective debate. The session supports intercultural awareness, analytical thinking, and appreciation of storytelling
traditions.
Telling a Story Without Words:
In small groups, participants create and perform short stories using only movement, facial expressions, gestures, or objects.
After each performance, the audience reflects on what they understood, highlighting the power of nonverbal storytelling as a
universal communication tool.
Creating a Mini Visual Story:
Teams design short digital stories on a chosen theme, collaborating on creative decisions and message clarity. The activity
develops digital skills, teamwork, and creativity while encouraging cross-cultural communication.
Introduction to Digital Storytelling Tools:
This workshop introduces participants to simple, accessible tools for digital storytelling, starting with a brief look at their role
in intercultural communication. Through guided demonstrations and small-group practice, participants will learn to use free
platforms such as Canva, Audacity, Pictory, and Storybird for creating visual, audio, and narrative content. The aim is to
build practical skills and confidence, encouraging experimentation and collaborative learning for later project phases.
Strategies for Making Stories More Inclusive and Accessible:
This session helps participants understand how to create inclusive and accessible digital stories. After an interactive
introduction to cultural representation, inclusive language, and accessibility, participants work in small groups to analyse
digital content and discuss related challenges. Using non-formal methods of group work and reflection, the aim is to develop
strategies for avoiding stereotypes, including diverse perspectives, and designing content for different audiences.
Music as a Universal Communication Tool:
This workshop explores how music expresses emotions, tells stories, and connects people beyond language. After a short
introduction with cultural examples, participants experiment with simple instruments and digital tools to create sounds and
rhythms, playfully discovering elements they can later use in digital stories.
Creating Soundscapes:
Building on the music workshop, teams design soundscapes to enhance their digital stories. After a brief demo on how
audio shapes mood and storytelling, they select and layer sounds to reflect emotions, settings, and narrative flow.
Facilitators provide guidance as groups develop draft soundscapes, enhancing creativity, teamwork, and technical skills
while showing how sound deepens intercultural communication.
The Value of Storytelling in Intercultural Dialogue:
Through an “interactive gallery walk,” participants reflect on storytelling’s role in promoting dialogue and empathy.
Responding to guiding questions, they share insights on posters and later discuss common themes, strengthening
understanding of storytelling as a tool for intercultural connection.

ongoing intercultural dialogue and teamwork. Using non-formal education methods, participants will continuously collaborate
in the design and development of their storytelling projects. They will be encouraged to share roles within the team (writer,
designer, editor) according to their strengths, while also rotating tasks to ensure that everyone develops a wide range of
skills and contributes equally to the final product. Communication will remain open, with facilitators encouraging a culture of
mutual respect and active listening. Regular feedback sessions will be held to help groups reflect on their dynamics, resolve
conflicts where they arise and strengthen collaboration. These sessions will also provide space to evaluate progress and
make collective decisions. In the follow-up phase, digital tools will support ongoing communication. Participants will share
the results of their projects within digital platforms and on social media, and will stay in touch to support each other in these
dissemination activities.
After the project, participants will be able to stay in touch: we plan to create an alumni group and will therefore keep the
project’s WhatsApp group active. The most important information about reimbursements will be sent both by email and via
WhatsApp, to make sure nothing gets lost. We will create an individual Google Drive folder for each participant, where they
can upload their invoices, flight tickets, and boarding passes. We will also share with them the photos and videos taken
during the project, supporting their dissemination efforts.

Project Design
Preparation, support and follow-up
How will you prepare the participants before the start of the activity (e.g. intercultural, linguistic, risk-prevention etc.) and
how will you support them during and after the activity?
Participants will receive a welcome pack by email before the project starts, including information about the project
objectives, the accommodation, the cultural background of the host country, the timetable, the participants ethical guidelines
and all the other important information. Before this, however, they will already receive the infopack, which is provided to
them during the application process. In order for us to properly prepare for the participants’ needs, they will fill out a Google
Form where they can indicate their food allergies, diet, arrival time, and all other practical information necessary for our
organization.
Participants will also receive the project’s ethical guidelines in advance, so they will already be familiar with them by the start
of the project. These include, for example, mutual respect, and a no-drug policy.
Team leaders play a key role in the project: we will hold an online preparatory meeting for them, during which we will
prepare them for their tasks, explain their responsibilities, present the program, and discuss what they need to prepare for.
They will serve as the primary bridge between the participants and the organizers before, but especially after, the project.
They will be the one who are organizing the tasks before the project among their national teams: for example, who brings
what to the national evenings.
On the first day, we will hold an introduction session where we will share with the participants the most important information
about the venue, sustainability practices, YouthPass, and the Erasmus+ program, as well as the rules of the project.
Preparation will also include basic risk prevention instructions, focusing on respect for local laws, health and safety
standards and responsible behaviour during group activities. During the activity, support will be provided through daily
briefings and small group reflections to monitor participants' well-being, learning progress and group dynamics. They can
also share here if they have any problems or would like to suggest any changes
Facilitators will ensure that everyone feels included and respected and will encourage collaboration and creative
participation through non-formal methods such as storytelling activities, group workshops and interactive discussions. At the
end of the activities, participants are guided through a final reflection phase to help them consolidate their learning
outcomes.
What measures will you put in place to ensure the safety and protection of participants?
Even before the project, with the help of the infopack, participants will be aware about the most important rules and
information. All participants will be required to have a European Health Insurance Card or private health insurance. Before
their arrival, we will ask participants—through a Google Form—about their food allergies, dietary needs, and any medical
conditions, so that we can be well prepared. Compliance with GDPR rules is also important for us, therefore the ethical
guidelines (which every participant will sign) will include consent for taking photos during the project. Anyone who has
concerns about this will be able to inform us separately.
To ensure the safety and protection of all participants, the activity will take place in a safe and accessible venue with clear
emergency procedures, first aid kits and staff available at all times. Team leaders will play a key role in supervising the
group, ensuring that safety protocols are followed and providing support where needed. They will act as a reference point for
participants throughout the project.
On the first day, the introductory session will cover the ethical guidelines of the participants, including anti-discrimination
measures, no drug policy, respectful communication, and behavioural guidelines to ensure a safe and inclusive
environment, creating an atmosphere of mutual trust and respect where participants feel comfortable expressing
themselves. This session will also introduce the team leaders and explain their roles. Attention is paid to participants' mental
wellbeing, especially during emotionally intense sessions such as storytelling or cultural discussions, and to cultural
differences, dietary requirements, allergies and any reported vulnerabilities. Regular check-ins with the group will help to
monitor wellbeing and address concerns as they arise.
In case of any emergency, the organizers will be always on-site and will be always available in case of any help needed.
What activities are foreseen after the end of the Youth Exchange? How will the participants follow-up on the activity?
After the exchange, participants will stay involved through follow-up activities designed to extend the impact of the project
beyond the mobility itself. One of the main tools for keeping in touch will be the WhatsApp group created during the project,
which will function as an alumni group after the exchange. It will be a space for sharing updates, exchanging ideas and
discussing on how participants are applying the skills they have acquired.
Participants will be involved through online dissemination. As for the online platforms it will be either Instagram, Facebook,
TikTok or YouTube, where they will share reflections, short videos, and updates inspired by their experiences. This will help
to inspire other young people, and showcase the project outcomes to a wider audience, as well as the Erasmus+ program,
especially among other young people in their communities, which is the most important target group when it comes to

dissemination. Facilitators will provide participants with simple guidelines to support their initiatives, and group leaders will
help coordinate follow-up communication.
Dissemination will take place on multiple levels:
- Participants will share their digital stories and reflections on their personal social media accounts, using specific hashtags
to increase visibility.
- Our organisation will ensure visibility through our official Instagram, Facebook and website, where we will publish the final
outcomes, including digital stories and reflective materials.
-The partner organisations will also share the results of the project through their own communication channels.
The final presentation during the exchange will act as a rehearsal for this follow-up, reinforcing ownership and responsibility.
In this way, the learning outcomes of the project will be reinforced and integrated into each participant's personal, social and
educational path.
As for the practical matters after the project, participants will need to upload their flight tickets, invoices, and boarding
passes to the Google Drive folder we created. After that, the travel reimbursement will be transferred to the participants’
bank accounts.
We will hold an online meeting with the partners after the project, where we will evaluate the project together, primarily
through the experiences of the team leaders, and discuss opportunities for future cooperation.
Recognition of learning outcomes
How will you support participants to be aware of what they have learned and which competences they have developed or
improved? Please remember to include the methods that support reflection and documentation of the learning outcomes in
the daily timetable of each activity.
Throughout the exchange, participants will be supported in multiple ways to become aware of their learning. One of the most
important way for this will be through reflection and group sharing moments at the end of each day, where they will be
invited to discuss what they have learned, helping them to process and give value to their experiences. These will happen
through guided questions to the participants in the small group. Questions will be like, for example: what was your key
takeaway for the day?; what did you learn today that you will use in the future?; what was something new for you that you
learned today?
In small, mixed, randomized intercultural groups, participants will use "learning journals" to record their progress, challenges
and personal insights. During the reflection time at the end of each day, they will take some time to write down their thoughts
and observations, guided by specific prompts from the facilitators, such as "What skill did I develop today?", "What was
challenging for me and how did I overcome it?". These journals will be structured but flexible, allowing participants to
express themselves through short texts or drawings. The aim is to make reflection a personal and engaging process, helping
them to track their progress throughout the exchange.
At the end of each activity, in the last 5-10 minutes, the group will reflect together, with guided questions from the facilitatory,
about the activity and what did they learnt, what was the aim of the activity. Facilitators will encourage them to link what they
have experienced to specific key competences, such as multilingualism, cultural awareness, digital literacy or sense of
initiative.
In addition, the Youthpass process will guide participants in identifying and documenting their learning outcomes throughout
the week, with group leaders and facilitators supporting participants to complete their certificates by helping them to reflect
on their development and express it in their own words. We will talk about YouthPass even on the first day, so participants
will be aware of the YouthPass process and they can reflect on their learning process throughout the week.
The Erasmus Programme promotes the use of instruments/certificates like Youthpass or Europass , to validate the
competences acquired by the participants during their experiences abroad. Will your project make use of such European
instruments/certificates?

Participant with fewer opportunities
Are there participants involved in the activities who face situations that make their participation in the activities more
difficult?
Yes
Which types of situations are these participants facing?
Economic barriers
If any, please explain the particular measures (accompanying person, reinforced preparation etc.) you will put in place to
cater for the specific needs of these participants and/or to support their participation.
The project aims to be as inclusive and accessible as possible, overcoming any barriers that may arise. We plan to prioritize
participants with fewer opportunities during the selection process, we will do this regarding the Hungarian participants and
will communicate this to our partner NGOs too. In particular, we will develop strategies for young people who may face
economic barriers, as some participants may come from disadvantaged backgrounds and be unable to cover travelling
costs. This could affect their ability to engage fully in project activities. If any participant facing economic barriers is unable
to purchase their flight ticket, they can let us know, and either we or the respective partner organization will buy it for them.
In this case, the travel reimbursement will be transferred directly to the partner. And since the project is about digital
storytelling, we do not want anyone to be excluded just because they cannot provide the necessary equipment. For
participants who do not have a laptop or smartphone, we will provide such devices. We will communicate in advance to
participants with fewer opportunities that the project is funded by Erasmus+ and the European Union, and therefore their
participation is free of charge – the focus is entirely on their learning.
We will also develop strategies for young people with disabilities, as some participants may have physical or sensory
limitations requiring specific adjustments to ensure equal participation. We are also committed to dealing with any other
potential challenges, including those related to cultural differences, given the project's intercultural nature. At the same time,
we actively combat any form of discrimination and promote an inclusive environment where everyone feels valued and
respected, regardless of their background, abilities, or personal circumstances. For participants with disabilities, we will
ensure that the venue is physically accessible and equipped to accommodate mobility aids. Materials will be provided in
accessible formats, which may include larger print, audio or other options as needed. If necessary, we will provide an
accompanying person to assist people with special needs. In addition, we will ensure that any other potential needs are
addressed as required, promoting an inclusive and supportive environment throughout the project. We will remain vigilant
against any form of discrimination and actively promote a culture of respect, equality and diversity within the group.
Virtual learning/Blended activities and use of virtual components
Do you foresee Virtual/Blended activities and/or the use of any virtual component, before, during or after the activity?
Yes
If yes, please describe them.
Yes, the project will include virtual components in all phases of the project:
-Before the activity, to facilitate introductions and group cohesion, participants will be invited to join a WhatsApp group
before the exchange. This will help them to get to know each other, share expectations and ask any questions about the
upcoming activities. Group leaders will take part in an online preparatory meeting. Google Forms will be used by the partner
organizations and us for the selection of the participants and email for the confirmation of the participation.
-During the project, virtual components will be minimal as the focus will be on face-to-face interaction. However, participants
will use digital tools such as Canva, Audacity and Pictory to create digital projects. These tools will enhance the creative
process and promote digital literacy. During the cultural nights, Kahoot and Mentimeter will be used for quizzes.
-After the end of the activities, the WhatsApp group will continue to function as an alumni group to help participants stay
connected, share their progress and discuss how they are applying their new skills in everyday life. In addition, platforms
such as Instagram, Facebook, YouTube and websites will be used to share experiences and outcomes, giving the project
visibility and allowing participants to share their work with a wider audience. For the reimbursement process, participants
will upload their tickets and boarding passes to the shared Google Drive folder of the project, as well as the pictures has
been taken during the project.

Environmental friendly practices
Will you include sustainable and environmental-friendly practices in your activities?
Yes
Please describe them and mention how will you raise the awareness of participants on these sustainable practices?
Yes, the project will incorporate sustainable and environmentally friendly practices to reduce its impact and raise awareness
among participants. Clear guidelines will be provided from the start on how to properly separate waste, including bins for
paper, plastic, organic waste and general rubbish. We will introduce these guidelines to the participants on the first day,
during the introductory session.
To minimise waste, we will provide reusable water cups, in order to reduce the use of single-use plastics and avoid
disposable items, including the cultural nights. There will be separated bins for paper, plastic and organic waste at the
venue. Meals will focus on local and seasonal produce wherever possible, reducing the environmental impact of food
transport. In terms of energy conservation, participants will be reminded to switch off lights and electronic devices when not
in use.
We will have a no-food-waste policy at the venue: we will monitor how much food has been wasted and will discuss them
with the chefs at the venue and will provide the portions accordingly. Of course, no one will be hungry, there will be always
option for more food if someone needed.
We will also promote green travel options. Participants will need to use public transport to get to the venue. Throughout the
project we will raise awareness of sustainable practices by integrating them into daily routines to help participants develop a
more conscious and responsible mindset, both during the project and beyond.

Project Management
In this section we want you to describe several aspects of how you will manage your project and make sure that the
participants have a high quality learning outcome. Please read the questions carefully and answer to all sub-questions.
How will you manage the project (agreements with partners etc.) and make sure that it is done in line with the Erasmus
Youth Quality Standards? You will find the quality standards further down in the application form.
The project will be managed by ensuring that all steps comply with the Erasmus+ Youth Quality Standards.
Formal partnership agreements will be established with all participating organisations before the project begins, defining the
roles, responsibilities and tasks of each partner. These agreements will cover the following: reimbursement rules,
dissemination tasks of the partners and the participants; participant support; selection criteria of the participants; role of the
team leaders. It is important to mention that, in addition to the agreement with the partners, there will also be an agreement
with the participants – the ethical guidelines – which will include, among other things, the participants’ commitment to active
participation, mutual respect, the exclusion of racism and sexism, and a no-drug policy. This will already be included in the
info pack so that all participants are aware of it in advance, and they will also sign this document.
Partner organizations will receive regular email updates, at multiple milestones of the project: when it comes to the
acceptance to the project; 3 months before the project, with the guidelines of the participant selection and the infopack;
when it comes to the team leaders meeting;
Each partner will designate a project coordinator who will be responsible for maintaining communication between their
participants and the organizers, reporting progress and addressing challenges. To ensure inclusion, we will make the
project accessible to young people from diverse backgrounds, including those facing barriers related to economic conditions
and disability. This will be the responsibility of the partner organizations, to select these participants, based on their local
network.
Moreover, the project complies with Erasmus+ Youth quality standards by prioritizing participant inclusion and diversity,
integrating environmentally sustainable practices, fostering digital learning, and promoting active engagement from both
participants and partner organizations. We will also actively combat discrimination and aiming to create a respectful
environment. We will promote environmental sustainability by adopting eco-friendly practices throughout the project, such
as reducing waste and using reusable items. Virtual components will be integrated to enhance cooperation and mobility,
including online meetings and collaborative digital tools. These will help maintain communication among partners and
ensure continuity in project management. Monitoring and evaluation will be conducted through continuous feedback
collection durint the project with reflection sessions and team leader meetings, ensuring the project remains on track and
identifying areas for improvement.
Participants will be informed about YouthPass from the first day of the project and will encouraged to complete Youthpass to
document their learning outcomes and ensure the recognition and validation of their skills. The learning process of the
participants will be supported through reflections sessions, reflection at the end of each session and their reflection journal.
How will you organise the practical and logistical part of the project (e.g. travel, accommodation, insurance, visa, social
security, mentoring and support, preparatory meetings with partners etc.)?
The activity will take place at Legányi Apartmanház, Eger, a comfortable and fully equipped venue that provides indoor and
outdoor spaces that are suitable for workshops, group work, and informal interaction. The accommodation is safe, clean and
accessible, with 3-4 bedded shared rooms and common areas that encourage community building. We have already
organized a project at this accommodation before, where we were convinced that it is a perfect project venue. We will be the
only group staying there, so we will not disturb anyone else. It is also important that the accommodation provides catering,
which takes this burden off our shoulders. The venue can be reached within 15 minutes from Eger train station, making it
easily accessible for all participants. Participants can reach Eger in 2 hours by train from Budapest-Keleti railway station.
Before the project starts, we will hold a preparatory online meeting with the team leaders. We will coordinate travel
arrangements with partner organisations to ensure choosing sustainable and cost-effective options for participants and
encourage the use of public transport or other eco-friendly travel methods. The participants have to send us their travel
proposals so we can confirm and make sure that they are in line with the rules. Participants will be required to purchase their
tickets, except for participants with fewer opportunities, where we or the partner organization can buy them. The infopack
will contain all the necessary information with the practical and logistical details, including the limits for tickets and the
recommended route from Budapest airport.
As for the visa arrangements, none of the participants from the partner countries need a visa to enter Hungary.
All participants must have a European Health Insurance Card or private health insurance. We will communicate this clearly
to the participants, both in the info pack and through the team leaders.
Team leaders will provide mentoring and support, guiding participants throughout the project. Daily check-ins will be
conducted to monitor participants' comfort and engagement during the reflection sessions. Moreover, participants can turn
to the group leaders or organizers in case they have any problems.

Partnerships
How and why did you choose your project partners? What experiences and competences will they bring to the project?
We built the partnerships based on several criteria. On the one hand, we chose partners whom we personally know: such as
the Danish informal group and the Estonian partner, with whom we have a good personal relationship and know that they
carry out quality youth work. On the other hand, it was also an important aspect to select partners from different regions of
Europe. This was successfully achieved, as Eastern Europe and the Baltics are represented through Estonia, Northern
Europe through Denmark, and Southern Europe through Italy, alongside Hungary, which represents Central Europe. In this
way, we believe we ensured that young people from different cultures and with diverse experiences will be present in the
project. This will create international teamwork with as diverse a group of participants as possible – which we consider
essential for intercultural storytelling and for the overall nature of the project.
We also aimed to create a mix of experienced and less experienced organizations within the partnership. This is why we
decided to involve an informal group as well, MindClub, so that a different perspective could be included in the cooperation,
compared to the classic youth NGOs. It is important to note that we know this informal group personally, as well as the NGO
connected to them: they send motivated young people to every project, and they themselves have taken part in numerous
Erasmus+ youth exchanges. They are familiar with how participant selection, preparation, and dissemination processes
work.
Our Estonian partner, Youth Senate Tallinn, is a true Erasmus+ veteran: they have already sent participants from Estonia to
more than 141 projects and have organized 17 youth exchanges themselves. As members of our association, we have also
learned a great deal from them personally, having previously taken part in a training course they held in Estonia. A shared
goal we both work towards is the democratization of Erasmus+ youth exchanges, so that more and more young people from
wider backgrounds can get to know these programs. In Estonia, they actively promote Erasmus+ opportunities in rural
regions.
Our Italian partner, CSV Lombardia, was found via the SALTO portal, and after an online meeting we decided together that
we would like to cooperate. Since then, they are participating in our youth exchange and we are also partners in theirs. They
are a locally active NGO, which was an important selection criterion for us, since involving young people with fewer
opportunities requires organizations that also actively work with youth on the local level. They hold an ESC Quality Label,
which for us is a guarantee of high-quality youth work.
As for us, we position ourselves in the middle of the experience spectrum compared to our partners. We believe this is a
good combination, as it allows for both new perspectives and relevant experience to be represented in the project. Each
partner, including ourselves, can grow through the project, and the mix of diverse participants and partners ensures the
project’s success and provides a solid foundation to build on.
How will you communicate with them?
During the planning of the project, we communicated with the partners via email. This will continue, as we want to ensure
that nothing gets lost, especially important documents such as the partnership agreement. Therefore, we will maintain email
communication with the partners.
Before the implementation of the project, two WhatsApp groups will be created: one for the participants and one for the team
leaders (a few weeks earlier than the participants’ group). Through these channels, we will be able to communicate instantly
with both participants and team leaders. These groups will remain active during the implementation and follow-up phases as
well.
Prior to the mobility, we will organize an online meeting for the team leaders. In this meeting, we will discuss the most
important practical information regarding the project: the accommodation, the timetable, the roles and responsibilities of the
team leaders, travel details, and we will also answer their questions.
After the project, we will hold an evaluation meeting with the partner organizations, relying primarily on the feedback of the
team leaders, and we will also discuss possibilities for further cooperation.
How will you monitor and coordinate their contribution?
Each partner will be aware of their tasks and responsibilities, which we will communicate to them before the project begins.
During the project, we will set milestones, and we expect our partners to communicate their progress on these milestones in
email, to ensure everything is running smoothly on their side as well. Such milestones will include participant selection, team
leader selection, inclusion of young people with fewer opportunities, purchase of participants’ tickets, online meeting with
team leaders, and reimbursements. In case they have any questions, they can always turn to us in email or in WhatsApp. It
will be the responsibility of us to confirm the travel plans of the participants before the youth exchange.
We will hold two online meetings with the partners: before the mobility, we will organize a preparatory online meeting for the
team leaders delegated by the partners, during which we will discuss their tasks and responsibilities, the necessary
preparations on their part, and the program. After the mobility, we will hold an evaluation meeting and also discuss possible
future cooperation.

Evaluation
How will you evaluate your project’s success? Which activities will you carry out in order to assess whether, and to what
extent, your project has reached its objectives and results?
The success of the project will be evaluated using both qualitative and quantitative methods, focusing on the extent to which
the objectives have been achieved. Throughout the project, facilitators will collect direct feedback from participants through
daily reflection sessions where young people can express how they feel, what they have learned and what challenges they
have faced. At the end of the project, participants will complete a final evaluation form with open-ended questions and rating
scales linked to the project's learning outcomes, such as teamwork, digital skills and intercultural awareness, in order to
assess impact. Facilitators and group leaders will also meet for internal evaluations to discuss what worked well and what
could be improved.
We will use pre- and post project surveys for the quantitative indicators. On the first day of the project, as well as on the last
day, we will ask the participants to complete a survey in order to assess their skills and knowledge level, and to be able to
measure the learning they themselves define. We set some quantitative targets: at least 80% of participants reporting an
improvement in their digital and intercultural skills, as measured by project questionnaires. In addition, we aim for at least
85% of participants to express satisfaction with the project in overall, based on the questions below.
We will ask the following questions regarding the project’s topic:
How confident do you feel in identifying and reflecting on common cultural myths or stereotypes?”
“How well do you understand the concept of digital storytelling and its potential to share intercultural experiences?”
“How skilled do you feel in using digital tools (e.g. video, audio, social media) to tell a story?”
“How confident are you in collaborating with people from different cultural backgrounds?”
“How aware are you of the role that intercultural dialogue and storytelling can play in building mutual understanding in
Europe?
And these, as more general questions:
“How confident do you feel working in a diverse, international team?”
“How aware are you of the opportunities offered by Erasmus+ for young people?”
“How confident are you in expressing your ideas in front of a group?”
“How much do you know about non-formal learning methods and their benefits?”
“How confident are you in applying what you learn here to your personal, academic, or professional life?”
We would like to see a 30% improvement in overall with the answers of the participants, as we will use Likert scale to make
the answers measurable.
As for the more tangible indicators, we would like to achieve ont he project:
Gender ratio: Balanced gender representation among participants (minimum 40–60% ratio ensured).
Participation rate in program activities: At least 95% of participants attend every program (target: 100%).
Feedback rate: 100% of participants complete the final evaluation questionnaire.
Number of created content pieces: A minimum of 45 media contents (e.g., posts, videos, stories) are produced during the
project, both by organizers and participants.
Reach: The project’s social media posts reach at least 1,800 people.
Number of interactions: The project’s social media posts generate at least 300 interactions (likes, shares, comments).
Qualitative objectives include observing a greater sense of inclusion and intercultural understanding within the group, as
evidenced by positive feedback during daily reflection sessions. We will also be looking for participants' ability to articulate
their learning experiences and how they plan to use the skills they have acquired in the future. In the follow-up phase,
informal interviews and social media interaction with participants will help to understand whether the project has had a
lasting impact on them.

Sustainability of the results
What will you do to make sure that your project continues to have effects also after it ends?
Every participants will have a mandatory dissemination: to share their experiences and digital stories on social media, using
images and short captions in multiple languages to reach a wider audience and raise awareness of key issues such as
inclusion, diversity and the power of storytelling, as well as raise awareness on the Erasmus+ program.They will act as
multipliers, continuing to spread the project's message and promote positive change in their communities.
The digital content created during the exchange will remain accessible online, serving as a lasting output that can be reused
or adapted in other project or youth contexts. To ensure a lasting impact, participants will act as ambassadors for the project
in their local communities after the exchange, sharing their experiences and new skills with peers, family members and local
youth groups, raising awareness of the importance of intercultural dialogue and creative digital storytelling, as well as on the
Erasmus+ program. They will be supported by us and by the partner organizations to organise in the local community local
events, presentations or workshops to showcase their digital work and inspire others to use storytelling as a tool for
inclusion. The final digital stories and learning outcomes will be published on the official Instagram, Facebook and website
of the host and partner organisations to ensure that the results remain visible and accessible.
We would like to build on the results and learning from this project together with our partners, and we aim to implement a
larger-scale project, likely a KA210, related to the project topic. A good relationship with the partners is already established,
and this project can lay the groundwork for a larger project in the future. We will have a closing, evaluation online meeting
after the project, to discuss this as well.
Are you planning measures to make sure that the results produced are used and beneficial to others beyond the project’s
lifetime? If yes, which ones?
Yes. The project will produce digital and educational materials that will be shared online and made freely available. These
outputs will be published on the social media channels and websites of the organisations involved, ensuring visibility beyond
the direct participants and enabling others to re-use and adapt them for their own purposes. We will also upload these to the
SALTO portal so it will be available to other youth workers all across Europe.
We want our participants to be ambassadors of the project and the Erasmus+ program, so they will be encouraged to share
their learning with their schools, groups or online communities, with a particular focus on raising awareness of inclusion,
intercultural dialogue and media literacy, as well as the Erasmus+ program. The digital nature of the outputs will ensure that
they remain accessible and relevant over time. Organisations will also present the outputs in future activities (workshops,
webinars, local events) and Erasmus+ applications, integrating the good practices developed during the exchange into their
regular work.
After the project, we plan to create an alumni group so that participants can stay in touch with each other as well as with the
project topic. To support this, we will keep the WhatsApp group active, where we will continue to share videos and articles
related to the project topic, as well as opportunities for young people, and we will encourage participants to do the same.
In the long term, we will mainly use feedback from our partner organizations, which work directly with young people, to
assess the skills and knowledge participants have gained during the project and how they are applying them.

Dissemination of project results
How will you make your project visible outside your organisation and partner organisations? How will you share its results
and success? With whom will you share the results?
To increase the visibility of the project, we will implement a dissemination strategy targeting different audiences, including
youth workers, educators and local communities interested in the project theme.
Our main target audience will be young people aged 18-30. We will use social media platforms such as Instagram,
Facebook and YouTube to share the progress, outputs and final results of the project. For this target group we are planning
not only professional content but also fun content, such as live updates from the project, reports on energizers, and
coverage of cultural evenings
We will use project specific hashtags to boost visibility and reach our target group, such as #interculturalmyths;
#digitalstorytelling; #erasmus+; #youthexchange
The digital stories created during the exchange will be posted on the organisations' official accounts, accompanied by
subtitles in different languages to reach a wider audience. Participants will share content during the project on their personal
profiles, extending the reach of the project in the communities of the participants. We will also create a dedicated section on
Square of Youth Association’s website to showcase the digital stories, reflections and outcomes. Partner organisations will
also feature the project on their social media channels.
As we mentioned before, we will upload the project materials to SALTO so the project will have a wider impact and visibility
than the project duration.
As for the visibility goals of the project, we are setting a minimum of 45 media contents, including as posts, videos, and
stories, are produced by both organizers and participants, as a goal. We aim the project’s social media posts to reach at
least 1,800 people. In total, these posts should generate at least 300 interactions, including likes, shares, and comments.
After the project, participants will be encouraged to present their digital stories in their local contexts, such as schools, youth
centres or community events, to share their experiences and raise awareness of intercultural storytelling as a tool for
inclusion. We will also organize an offline dissemination event in Budapest, targeting young people aged 18–30. The aim of
this event will be to share what happened during the project, as well as to present Erasmus+ youth exchanges as an
opportunity for learning and traveling. Besides this, we will also hold a short workshop in the topic of the youth exchange. We
will also invite the Hungarian participants of the project to this event so they can share their experiences.
Using a combination of online and offline dissemination methods, we will ensure that the results and successes of the
project reach a wide and diverse audience, inspiring similar initiatives and promoting long-term impact.
How will you involve participants in such activities?
From the beginning of the exchange, participants will be informed about the importance of sharing the results and raising
awareness about the key issues of the project. They will have a mandatory dissemination task: to share a post on Facebook,
Instagram or on TikTok, including their personal experiences and/or digital stories on their own social media profiles, using
project-specific hashtags and tagging the organisations involved. With this, we would like to reach young people in the
participants’ communities who may not have taken part in an Erasmus+ project before, or may not even have heard about it.
In order to support the participants’ dissemination activities, we will upload the photos and videos taken during the project to
a shared Google Drive folder, which will be made available to all participants.
In addition, participants will also naturally promote the project and the Erasmus+ program in their communities through word
of mouth, which is one of the most effective methods.
During the exchange, they will receive practical tips on how to create content that effectively communicates their learning
outcomes. This will enable them to take an active role in promoting the project.
After the exchange, participants will be encouraged to act as project ambassadors by sharing their digital stories in their
local communities. To support this, they will be provided with simple guidelines and materials to help them feel more
confident in sharing their experiences. To strengthen their involvement, participants will be asked to reflect on their own
communication skills and how they can use them in the future.
